claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm responsible for. His name is Alex, he's 8 years old and lives with me here in the UK. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know that Alex is 8 years old and lives with you in the UK.

I have a few more questions to help determine your eligibility. First, is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation arranged by the local council?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually Alex has been in hospital. He's been there for a while now - a few months dealing with a medical issue. But I'm still very much involved in his care and supporting him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, thank you for sharing that. This is an important detail for your Child Benefit eligibility.

Can you tell me more specifically how long Alex has been in hospital? Has it been more than 12 weeks, and if so, are you regularly spending money on him (such as for clothes, toys, treats, or other items)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been right around 12 weeks now. And yes, I am still spending money on him - I buy him clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks and things like that when I visit. I'm still covering those kinds of expenses for him even though he's in hospital.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Just to clarify - when you say "right around 12 weeks," has it been exactly 12 weeks, or has it gone over 12 weeks (for example, 13 weeks or more)?

This timing is important because it affects whether the hospital stay impacts your Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been exactly 12 weeks, not over that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have enough information now to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 8 years old and lives with you in the UK
- He has been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks (not exceeding the 12-week threshold)
- You are regularly spending money on him during his hospital stay

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- Alex is 8 years old and lives with you in the UK, which meets the basic eligibility requirements
- While he's been in hospital for 12 weeks, your Child Benefit is only affected if a child is in hospital for **more than** 12 weeks
- Since it's been exactly 12 weeks (not exceeding the threshold), you remain entitled to Child Benefit
- Additionally, you're regularly spending money on Alex (clothes, magazines, snacks), which would qualify as an exception even if the hospital stay went beyond 12 weeks

You can claim Child Benefit for Alex. If his hospital stay does extend beyond 12 weeks, make sure to inform HMRC, but you should still remain eligible as long as you continue regularly spending money on him.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and lives with you in the UK, which meets the basic eligibility criteria (responsible for a child under 16 living in the UK). While Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks, the rules state that Child Benefit payments are affected only if a child is in hospital for MORE than 12 weeks. Since Alex has been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks (not exceeding this threshold), you remain entitled to Child Benefit. Additionally, you are regularly spending money on him during his hospital stay, which would be a qualifying exception even if the 12-week threshold were exceeded. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is responsible for one child, Alex (age 8), who lives with them in the UK. Alex has been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks. Since the hospital stay has not exceeded the 12-week threshold (payments are only affected after MORE than 12 weeks), and the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit.

While Alex has been in hospital for 12 weeks, the rules state that Child Benefit payments are affected only if a child is in hospital for MORE than 12 weeks. Since Alex has been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks (not exceeding this threshold), you remain entitled to Child Benefit. Additionally, you are regularly spending money on him during his hospital stay, which would be a qualifying exception even if the 12-week threshold were exceeded. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since the hospital stay has not exceeded the 12-week threshold (payments are only affected after MORE than 12 weeks), and the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex, the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit.
